verses.commentary: Approaching Jerusalem via Bethphage on Mount of Olives — east of the city, the place from which the Messiah was expected to come (Zechariah 14:4). Jesus sends two disciples ahead.
Donkey and her colt tied. Detailed pre-arrangement — either supernatural foresight or prior arrangement. The mission is precise.
'The Lord has need of them' — authoritative requisition. The animals are returned. Jesus' kingship is enacted in microcosm.
Fulfillment formula yet again — even the entry mode is scripted.

Zechariah 9:9 quoted: 'Tell daughter Zion, your king is coming, humble (praus, πραΰς), mounted on a donkey, on a colt, foal of a beast of burden.' Anti-triumphal triumphal entry. The conquering king on a war-horse this is not.
Disciples execute the plan. Obedience to detail.
Donkey and colt; cloaks placed on them. Jesus mounts (Matthew's phrasing perhaps reads the two-animal arrangement literally from Zechariah's parallelism).
Crowd spreads cloaks on the road (royal welcome — compare 2 Kings 9:13) and cuts branches. A spontaneous coronation procession.

'Hosanna' (hosanna, ὡσαννά, Hebrew 'save now,' from Psalm 118:25) — once liturgical petition, now praise-exclamation. 'Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord' (Psalm 118:26 — pilgrim psalm). 'Hosanna in the highest!' The crowd recognizes Davidic claim.
Whole city stirred (eseisthe, ἐσείσθη — shaken, earthquake-language). 'Who is this?' Jerusalem's question echoes the magi's 'Where is the king of the Jews?' (2:2). The city has not learned.
Crowds answer: 'The prophet Jesus from Nazareth.' Prophet — but not yet Messiah on their lips. Galilean origin emphasized — provincial credentials.
Temple courts. Jesus drives out (exebale, ἐξέβαλεν) sellers and buyers, overturns money-changers' tables, dove-sellers' seats. Prophetic action: judgment dramatized.

Isaiah 56:7 + Jeremiah 7:11 combined. 'My house shall be called a house of prayer' — but 'you have made it a den of robbers.' Robbery = exploiting pilgrims via currency-exchange surcharges and forced commerce. The temple has become extortion infrastructure.

Blind and lame come to him in the temple — categories barred from temple service (Leviticus 21:18-21; 2 Samuel 5:8). Jesus heals them in the temple itself. The Messiah's temple welcomes those the old order excluded.
Chief priests and scribes indignant at miracles and at children shouting 'Hosanna to the Son of David.' Their hostility extends to children's worship.
Jesus quotes Psalm 8:2 (LXX): 'Out of the mouths of infants and nursing babies you have prepared praise.' Children's praise is divinely arranged. The Messiah validates their voices.
Leaves the city for Bethany — overnight refuge with Mary, Martha, Lazarus' household. The pattern: city by day, Bethany by night.
Morning return. Jesus hungry. Mundane detail framing the next prophetic act.

Fig tree with leaves but no fruit. Jesus pronounces: 'May no fruit ever come from you again.' Immediate withering. The fig tree symbolizes Israel (Hosea 9:10; Jeremiah 8:13) — leafy appearance, fruitless reality. Acted prophecy against the temple system.
Disciples marvel: 'How did the fig tree wither so quickly?' They see the wonder but miss the symbol.
'If you have faith and do not doubt — you can do this and more.' Mountain-moving language again (compare 17:20). 'This mountain' may even gesture toward the Temple Mount.
Whatever you ask in prayer, believing — you will receive. Confidence anchored in faith, not formula. Prayer is the petitionary correlate of mountain-moving faith.
Back in the temple. Chief priests and elders challenge: 'By what authority do you do these things, and who gave you this authority?' Hierarchical credential-check after his temple action.
Jesus countersuit: 'I will ask you one question. Answer it, and I will tell you.' He sets the trap.
'John's baptism — from heaven or from men?' They calculate: if heaven, they should have believed John; if men, the crowds will riot. Honest answer is impossible for them.
Fear of the crowd, not regard for truth, governs their reasoning. The crowds held John a prophet. The political risk is real.
'We do not know.' Calculated cowardice. Jesus refuses to disclose his authority. He has exposed theirs as captive to expediency.
Parable: man with two sons. The didactic move shifts to story.
First son refuses, then repents (metameletheis, μεταμεληθεὶς — change of mind) and goes.
Second son agrees politely ('I will, sir' — kyrie, κύριε) but does not go. Smooth talk, no action.
'Which did the father's will?' The first. 'Tax collectors and prostitutes enter the kingdom of God ahead of you.' The first son represents repentant sinners; the second, the religious leaders.
John came in the way of righteousness — you did not believe. Tax collectors and prostitutes did. Even after seeing this, you did not repent. Witnessed repentance failed to evoke repentance.
Another parable: landowner planted vineyard, built tower and winepress, leased to tenants. Echo of Isaiah 5:1-7 — God's vineyard is Israel. The story is Israel's history.
Harvest time. Owner sends servants to collect his share.
Tenants beat one, killed another, stoned a third. Prophets' fate dramatized (compare 23:37; 2 Chronicles 24:21).
More servants sent — same treatment. The pattern of prophetic rejection escalates with each delegation.
Finally sends his son: 'They will respect my son.' The son is the climactic envoy. Christology embedded — the Son in distinction from servants.
Tenants conspire: 'This is the heir; let us kill him and have his inheritance.' Cynical calculation. The cross plot summarized in advance.
Throw him out of the vineyard and kill him. Outside the vineyard = outside the city walls (compare Hebrews 13:12 — Jesus crucified outside the gate).
'What will the owner do to those tenants?' Jesus invites them to deliver their own verdict.
They answer: 'He will put those wretches to a wretched death and lease the vineyard to others who will give him the fruits.' The leaders pronounce judgment on themselves.
Psalm 118:22 quoted: 'The stone rejected has become the cornerstone.' The rejected son becomes the foundation. Vindication after rejection.
'The kingdom will be taken from you and given to a nation (ethnei, ἔθνει) producing its fruits.' Transfer announced. The new people of God will be a fruit-producing community.
'Whoever falls on this stone will be broken; on whomever it falls, it will crush.' Stone-Christology: two ways of failure-encounter with Christ, neither painless.
Chief priests and Pharisees perceive he speaks of them. They grasp the parable's target.
They seek to arrest him but fear the crowd, which holds him a prophet. Crowd pressure is again the restraining factor. The plot ripens but waits.
